gm.name: John Spytek
gm.title: General Manager
gm.previous_role: Assistant General Manager, Tampa Bay Buccaneers

career_path.role: Pro Personnel Intern → College Scouting Assistant → College/Pro Scout
career_path.mentor: Tom Heckert Jr., Andy Reid, Howie Roseman environment

career_path.role: Director of Player Personnel → VP of Player Personnel → Assistant General Manager

scouting_tree.philosophical_lineage: Eagles scouting foundation (Andy Reid/Tom Heckert) → Denver Broncos Elway-era national scouting → Jason Licht's Tampa Bay 'draft-and-develop, character-first' model. The strongest and most defining influence is Licht's Tampa Bay system, where Spytek spent 9 years and rose from Director of Player Personnel to Assistant GM. The 'I Am That Man' character-first evaluation process was developed during Spytek's tenure. Philadelphia Eagles roots show in his broad scouting background (both pro and college tracks). Elway-era Denver exposure during a championship window provided experience evaluating veteran rosters. Spytek also came up working with Andy Reid, Tom Heckert, John Harbaugh, Mike Holmgren, and Gary Kubiak across his stops.
scouting_tree.breakpoints: Spytek appears more willing to trade down and accumulate picks than Licht historically was. In his first draft, he traded down twice in Round 2 to acquire additional Day 2 and Day 3 picks — a more aggressive trade-back approach than Licht, who traditionally stayed put and picked BPA. Also showed willingness to draft a RB at pick 6 (Jeanty), something the Licht/Tampa tree rarely did (RB not a premium position there until Bucky Irving in Round 4). Spytek may be less positionally rigid than Licht while keeping the character-first core intact. He also explicitly stated he disagrees with pure BPA drafting, favoring a 'team-building' approach that factors in positional value — a slight departure from his predecessor in LV (Telesco) but consistent with Licht's philosophy.
scouting_tree.lineage_weight_notes: Only 1 draft as Raiders GM. Scouting tree (primarily Licht/Tampa Bay) is the strongest behavioral predictor. After the 2026 draft, lineage weight should drop to 0.45–0.50. His own stated philosophy and limited draft history get the remaining 0.30 weight.

key_influencers.name: Klint Kubiak
key_influencers.title: Head Coach
key_influencers.known_biases: Offensive-minded from Shanahan coaching tree. Runs wide-zone scheme with heavy play-action. Values fullbacks, two-back sets, athletic OL who can move laterally. Wants timing-based, disciplined QBs. Prioritizes run game and OL. Recently won Super Bowl LX as Seattle's OC. First-time HC, so likely collaborative with Spytek rather than dominant.

key_influencers.name: Tom Brady
key_influencers.title: Minority Owner
key_influencers.known_biases: Former college teammate of Spytek at Michigan. Spytek has confirmed Brady is involved in 'any big decision' and is 'part of the process.' Brady's primary influence is at the QB position — Spytek said 'we'd have to be fools not to involve him' when evaluating QBs. Brady reportedly endorsed 6th-round QB Cam Miller pick in 2025. Brady was involved in the decision to pass on Shedeur Sanders and Sam Darnold. Emphasizes preparation, discipline, competitive spirit. Not in the building daily (Fox broadcasting obligations) but in constant communication.

key_influencers.name: Brian Stark
key_influencers.title: Assistant General Manager
key_influencers.known_biases: Former Denver Broncos Director of College Scouting (since 2016). Brings Broncos scouting DNA. Previous relationship with Spytek from Denver days.

key_influencers.name: Anthony Patch
key_influencers.title: Senior Personnel Executive
key_influencers.known_biases: 23-year veteran from Philadelphia Eagles scouting department (longest-tenured scout there). Came up with Spytek under Andy Reid/Tom Heckert in Philly 2005–2009. Brings Eagles analytical scouting approach. Reunion hire that deepens Philly scouting roots in LV.

key_influencers.name: Mark Davis
key_influencers.title: Owner
key_influencers.known_biases: Has empowered Spytek and Brady to lead football decisions. Stated he wanted an offensive-minded coach because of the #1 pick. Not a football mind himself but has historically been involved in coaching hires. Said Spytek will lead football operations 'in close collaboration' with Brady.

front_office.power_dynamics: Spytek has the final say on personnel and draft decisions, but operates in close collaboration with Brady (minority owner) and Kubiak (HC). Davis has empowered the Spytek-Brady tandem to guide all football decisions. Brady is not in the building daily but is consulted on all major decisions, especially QB evaluation. Kubiak reports to Spytek and Brady. This is a collaborative front office with three power centers (Spytek/Brady/Kubiak), but Spytek holds the pen on draft day. Some 2025 reporting suggested confusion about who was making certain calls, indicating the power structure was still being defined.
front_office.owner_interference: medium — Mark Davis is not a football decision-maker himself, but he elevated Tom Brady to a de facto co-decision-maker role. Brady's influence is atypical for a minority owner. Davis has fired 4 HCs and 3 GMs since 2021, suggesting impatience. His involvement is more through the Brady proxy than direct football meddling.

draft_philosophy.stated_philosophy: Team-building approach that rejects pure BPA and pure need. Prioritizes 'quality humans and competitors' who love football. Understands positional value. Believes in draft-and-develop model learned in Tampa Bay.
draft_philosophy.actual_philosophy: In his only draft (2025), took RB Ashton Jeanty at 6 — arguably BPA but also the team's biggest offensive need (worst rushing attack in NFL). Then traded down twice in Round 2 to accumulate picks, suggesting comfort with a range of players rather than needing one specific guy. Took WR Jack Bech (need), CB Darien Porter (need, but raw/developmental), and loaded up on OL and DL depth — both huge needs. The picks leaned heavily need-driven given the roster's state. His quote about Ron Wolf warning against drafting for need suggests he aspires to BPA but the roster reality forced need-filling. The Tampa Bay lineage suggests a BPA lean at premium positions with need-filling on Day 2/3.
draft_philosophy.philosophy_mismatch_notes: Claims to avoid pure need drafting, citing Ron Wolf's advice. However, every single 2025 pick addressed a clear team need. This is partly circumstantial — the roster had holes everywhere — but still notable. His Tampa Bay mentor Licht operated similarly: BPA in theory, but schematic fit and character always factored in. Spytek explicitly said 'understanding the value of certain positions' is key, signaling a positional value overlay on top of BPA.

trade_triggers: Deep cluster of similarly-graded players available, making trade-down cost minimal
Opportunity to convert 2 picks into 4+ picks (as he did in 2025 Round 2)
Board value drop where next-tier players are still available 10–20 picks later
Possible QB trigger — stated willingness to be aggressive for franchise QB
trade_behavior.draft_capital_philosophy: Strong preference for accumulating Day 2 picks. Turned 2 picks into 4 picks (plus Day 3 additions) in his first draft by trading down twice in Round 2. Also traded a 3rd-round pick for Geno Smith pre-draft. Stated 'the more picks the better' and believed 'the value was in Rounds 2 and 3.' Tampa Bay under Licht was a draft-capital hoarder who rarely traded premium picks for veteran players. Spytek appears to carry this forward. Mid-season, traded Jakobi Meyers for a 4th and 6th round pick — willing to sell assets for future capital.

position_avoidance: No clear positional avoidance yet given only 1 draft. Tampa Bay lineage suggests LB is deprioritized early (White was exception). RB was taken at 6 overall which is atypical for Tampa tree — driven by Pete Carroll's influence and historic Jeanty value.
position_premium: OL heavily prioritized — 4 of 11 picks were OL/DL, and Spytek said he'd have 'felt bad' leaving without linemen. Tampa Bay history under Licht: 5 of last 6 first-round picks were trenches. DT also premium (Vita Vea, Calijah Kancey). Spytek explicitly stated 'understanding the value of certain positions' with OL/DL emphasis. In 2026, QB is locked in at pick 1 (Mendoza). OL is top remaining priority per multiple reports.
positional_value.notes: Tampa Bay's positional value hierarchy under Licht: OT/DT at the top, CB in Round 2 regularly, WR in mid-rounds, RB in mid-rounds. Spytek likely carries similar values but adapted to Raiders' extreme needs.

QB.archetype_summary: Franchise-caliber leader who loves football with maniacal preparation habits. Process-oriented, disciplined passer who can execute a timing-based, play-action-heavy scheme. Not a pure runner but needs enough mobility to extend plays. Arm talent matters but processing speed, accuracy, and competitive fire are non-negotiable. Brady's influence elevates the character and preparation bar to an extreme level. Spytek described his ideal QB at the 2026 combine as 'a leader, tough as hell, somebody that loves to play football, maniacal preparer.' Willingness to sit a rookie behind a veteran mentor.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Arm strength matters. Mobility about pocket movement and play extension, not designed runs. Tom Brady's influence means pure arm talent and accuracy are weighted heavily.

playing_style.primary_style: Timing-based pocket passer who can execute play-action and bootlegs in Kubiak's Shanahan-tree offense. Must process quickly and deliver with rhythm.
least_important_traits: Elite deep ball (nice-to-have, not required in this scheme)
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Brady-caliber competitive fire and preparation; leadership that changes locker room culture

college_production.production_floor: Must have been a multi-year starter. Values winning — Mendoza won national championship.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers proven winners with ascending production curves
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — Brady's influence means they evaluate the person and process, not just stats

examples: 2025 6.213 — Tommy Mellott, Montana State: Dual-threat FCS QB drafted as position-flex (likely WR convert). Shows willingness to find versatile athletes.

2025 6.215 — Cam Miller, North Dakota State: FCS championship-winning QB. Brady reportedly endorsed. Miller cited being told Raiders 'want people that are winners and accustomed to winning games.' Spytek values winning culture.

2026 1.1 (projected) — Fernando Mendoza, Indiana: Heisman winner, national champion. Kubiak has been studying his film. Spytek indicated he wants to 'limit pressure' on a young QB and possibly sit him behind a veteran.

QB.confidence_note: No premium QB drafted yet. 2026 pick 1 (Mendoza) will be defining. Profile inferred from Brady's influence, Spytek's stated preferences, Kubiak's scheme needs, and the character-first Tampa Bay philosophy.

EDGE.archetype_summary: Versatile, high-motor edge rusher who can play in multiple alignments. Tampa Bay influence suggests valuing versatile pass rushers who can stand up or put hand in dirt, with emphasis on athleticism and motor. Scheme is switching to 3-4 under new DC Rob Leonard, meaning OLB/EDGE hybrid types who can rush and drop are especially valued. Given Maxx Crosby's presence (elite pass rusher), complementary EDGE picks may be lower priority unless Crosby is traded.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No confirmed threshold from own picks
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Explosiveness (vert, broad) and motor. Tampa Bay drafted YaYa Diaby (6-3, 254, athletic, high motor) and Shaq Barrett (signed as FA — undersized but relentless). Calijah Kancey shows comfort with undersized disruptors.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Not a pure speed rusher requirement — motor and versatility more important

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Versatility — able to rush from multiple alignments. 3-4 scheme demands EDGE players who can stand up and drop into coverage
deal_breakers: One-dimensional rushers who can't set the edge
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite first step combined with high motor — see Tampa's love of YaYa Diaby and the Jason Pierre-Paul trade

college_production.production_floor: Unknown from own picks — Hemingway had 9.5 sacks over 3 years
college_production.production_trajectory: Tampa favored ascending production (Diaby broke out as a senior)
college_production.context_sensitivity: Moderate — willing to take rotational DL players who project

examples: 2025 4.135 — Tonka Hemingway, DT/EDGE, South Carolina: 9.5 sacks in final 3 years. Versatile — 'can play three technique, five technique, big end. He's got interior rush ability.' Sub-5.0 40 time. Tested well athletically. Versatility was the selling point.

EDGE.confidence_note: Only 1 DL pick that profiles as a pass-rushing DT/EDGE hybrid. Tampa Bay tree heavily valued DL (Vea, Kancey, Diaby, JPP trade) — expect Spytek to invest here. 3-4 scheme change affects archetype targets significantly.

OT.archetype_summary: Athletic, versatile offensive lineman who can move laterally in a zone-blocking scheme. Kubiak's Shanahan-tree offense requires OTs who can reach-block, execute outside zone, and pass protect with footwork rather than pure anchor. Values durability and positional flexibility (can play multiple OL spots). Willing to take FCS or small-school tackles if the athleticism and movement skills are right.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: Likely 33"+ based on Tampa history (Wirfs had elite length)
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Lateral agility and footwork for zone scheme. Movement skills matter more than bench press.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: N/A — but athletic testing matters for zone scheme fit

playing_style.primary_style: Zone-blocking tackle who can reach and seal in outside zone. Must be able to mirror in pass protection with feet rather than pure power.
least_important_traits: Elite anchor against power rushers (scheme doesn't demand it as primary)
deal_breakers: Stiff, non-athletic linemen who can't move laterally
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: True left tackle potential with elite movement skills

college_production.production_floor: Prefers durability/start count — Rogers had 55 consecutive starts
college_production.context_sensitivity: Willing to take FCS players (Charles Grant from William & Mary) if traits are right

examples: 2025 3.98 — Caleb Rogers, OT/G, Texas Tech: 6-5, 312. 55 consecutive starts. Iron man with experience, expected to move inside to guard. Big and experienced but 'still raw' — developmental projection bet.

2025 3.99 — Charles Grant, OT, William & Mary: 6-5. FCS prospect. Spytek said he was 'a perfect fit for the scheme' and liked his left tackle experience. Small-school pedigree shows Spytek is comfortable with non-Power 5 players if traits are right.

OT.confidence_note: 2 OT/OL picks in Day 2 of first draft, plus Tampa Bay's heavy OL investment (Wirfs, Graham Barton, Cody Mauch). Kubiak's wide-zone scheme is the key schematic driver.

IOL.archetype_summary: Athletic interior lineman who can pull, reach-block, and execute zone concepts. Versatility across guard/center is valued. Kubiak's system demands OL who move well laterally. Tampa Bay drafted athletic IOL types like Cody Mauch (NDSU, converted from tight end — extremely athletic) and Graham Barton (Duke, center). Power is secondary to movement skills in this scheme.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Short shuttle and 3-cone for agility. Broad jump for explosiveness. Zone scheme demands athletic guards.

playing_style.primary_style: Zone-blocking guard/center who can reach, pull, and get to the second level
least_important_traits: Pure power/anchor — not the primary requirement
deal_breakers: Slow feet, inability to execute reach blocks
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Center-guard versatility with elite movement skills

examples: 2025 3.98 — Caleb Rogers, Texas Tech: Drafted as OT but expected to move to guard. 55 straight starts, 6-5/312, but needs to improve balance and pad level. Movement skills are the bet.
Tampa Bay precedent: Cody Mauch (2023, NDSU, 2nd round — converted TE, elite athlete), Graham Barton (2024, 1st round — center). Both highly athletic zone-scheme fits.

IOL.confidence_note: Tampa Bay tree provides strong signal for IOL preferences. Kubiak's zone scheme is the key variable.

DT.archetype_summary: Disruptive interior presence who can penetrate and rush the passer from the interior. Tampa Bay influence is massive here — Licht drafted both Vita Vea (330-lb nose) and Calijah Kancey (280-lb undersized penetrator). Spytek's regime in Tampa showed comfort with both archetypes. New 3-4 scheme under Rob Leonard may demand a traditional nose who can two-gap, plus penetrating 3-techniques. Versatility valued — Hemingway and Pegues drafted in 2025 both play multiple positions along DL.

physical_profile.weight_range: 280–340 (wide range reflecting both penetrator and nose archetypes)
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Interior quickness (10-yard split). Sub-5.0 40 time for DTs is a positive signal (Hemingway cited as testing well with sub-5.0). Tampa Bay precedent: Kancey was 6-1, 280 — undersized but elite burst.

playing_style.primary_style: Versatile interior defender who can play multiple spots along the front. Both penetrating 3-technique and run-stuffing nose types valued.
least_important_traits: Size alone — Kancey at 280 and Vea at 330 both drafted in Round 1
deal_breakers: One-dimensional run stuffers with no pass rush
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite interior disruption — Kancey went 19th despite size concerns because of his pass-rush dominance

examples: 2025 4.135 — Tonka Hemingway, South Carolina: 5 seasons. 9.5 sacks in 3 years. 'Can play three technique, five technique, big end. Interior rush ability. Ran sub 5.0.' Versatility and athleticism were selling points.

2025 6.180 — JJ Pegues, Ole Miss: 300+ lbs with exceptional agility. 7 rushing TDs as wildcat QB. 'Unique athletic traits' — played DT, DE, nose, plus fullback/wildcat QB. 74.6 PFF defensive grade plus 92.3 PFF offensive grade.
Tampa Bay precedent: Vita Vea (2018, 1st round — 6-4, 347, dominant run-stuffer), Calijah Kancey (2023, 1st round — 6-1, 280, elite pass-rush DT). Both extremes of the size spectrum.

DT.confidence_note: 2 DT picks in first draft, plus strong Tampa Bay DT draft history. Versatility theme is clear.

CB.archetype_summary: Long, athletic, developmental cornerback with size and speed to match NFL receivers. Comfortable taking raw prospects with elite physical tools and limited starting experience if the athletic profile is exceptional. The Darien Porter pick defines this archetype — 6-3, 4.30 speed, 33" arms, but only 7 career starts as a converted WR. Traits-over-production philosophy at CB. Ball skills from WR background are a plus. Special teams contribution expected early.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed first — Porter ran 4.30 with a 9.99 RAS. Length and speed combination is the priority. 36.5" vertical, 131" broad jump, 6.71 3-cone. This is a 'freak athlete' CB philosophy.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.45 or faster strongly preferred — Porter at 4.30 sets a high bar

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Zone-capable with size to press. Pete Carroll/Seattle influence (Cover 3 with long, physical corners) influenced 2025 pick. New DC Rob Leonard's scheme TBD but likely continues zone emphasis.
least_important_traits: Polished press-man technique (willing to develop this)
deal_breakers: Short, slow corners without elite athleticism
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Generational athletic testing — Porter's 9.99 RAS clearly moved him up their board. Spytek/Carroll valued the physical tools over the limited experience.

college_production.production_floor: Very low — Porter had 3 INTs and only 7 starts in his career. Production clearly secondary to traits at CB.
college_production.production_trajectory: Late bloomer accepted — Porter didn't start until his 6th college season
college_production.context_sensitivity: Adjusted for limited opportunity — valued flashes and athletic potential over volume stats

examples: 2025 3.68 — Darien Porter, CB, Iowa State: 6-3, 195, 4.30 40, 33 1/8" arms, 36.5" vert, 131" broad, 6.71 3-cone (9.99 RAS — 4th best CB ever tested). WR-to-CB convert. Only 7 career starts. Special teams demon (5 blocked kicks). Bypassed more polished CBs (Shavon Revel, Nohl Williams) for Porter — strong signal that combine testing and length are paramount.

CB.confidence_note: Only 1 CB drafted but the pick is extremely telling — traits-over-production, length-and-speed obsession, comfort with developmental profiles. Tampa Bay also drafted CBs in Round 2 regularly (Carlton Davis, Morrison, Parrish in 2025).

S.archetype_summary: Insufficient data from own picks to establish archetype. Tampa Bay's Antoine Winfield Jr. (drafted 2020) provides the lineage signal — explosive, hard-hitting, versatile safety who can play both box and single-high. Winfield was a compact, physical player with elite instincts. Spytek's anecdote about T.J. Ward — 'the way he could strike ball carriers and blockers in a body as compact as his' — reveals an affinity for physical, explosive safeties. Expect preference for physical, competitive safeties with versatility.

physical_profile.height_range: 5-10 to 6-2 (Tampa Bay drafted both Winfield at 5-9 and larger safeties)
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Explosiveness (vertical, broad). Toughness and striking ability over pure range.

playing_style.primary_style: Versatile, physical safety who can play multiple roles
least_important_traits: Pure deep-field range (if it comes at expense of physicality)
deal_breakers: Soft tackling, lack of competitive fire
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite explosiveness combined with physicality — T.J. Ward profile

examples: No safety drafted in 2025. Spytek re-signed UDFA Isaiah Pola-Mao, showing he values young players who 'stepped up' when given opportunity.
Lineage evidence: T.J. Ward (Cleveland, Spytek era) — 5-10, 200, explosive and physical. Antoine Winfield Jr. (Tampa, 2020 2nd round) — 5-9, 203, versatile and instinctive.

S.confidence_note: No safety drafted in sole draft. Profile entirely inferred from scouting tree and Spytek's stated T.J. Ward admiration.

LB.archetype_summary: Run-stopping, high-character linebacker who loves football. Day 3 investment priority — not a premium position. 3-4 scheme shift under Leonard may elevate LB importance going forward. Tampa Bay deprioritized LB in early rounds (Devin White in 2019 was the exception). Spytek took LB Cody Lindenberg with his final pick in 2025 — good run defender, Senior Bowl standout, high-character.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Instincts and production more important than combine testing at LB

least_important_traits: Elite coverage ability (nice-to-have, not required for Day 3 LBs)
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: N/A — not a position they reach on

examples: 2025 7.222 — Cody Lindenberg, LB, Minnesota: Good run defender, Senior Bowl standout. Final pick of the draft. Addressed the position late, suggesting it's not a premium investment for Spytek.

LB.confidence_note: Only 1 LB drafted (Day 3). 3-4 scheme change may increase LB priority in 2026. Tampa Bay lineage treated LB as mid-round or later priority outside of Devin White.

WR.archetype_summary: Physical, competitive, reliable receivers who love football. Not chasing a prototypical #1 outside receiver — Spytek stated at the 2026 combine he doesn't believe in building around a clear-cut WR1, preferring a 'committee' approach. Values route-running technique, hands, competitiveness, and willingness to block. Willing to take deep threats on Day 3 for field-stretching. Kubiak's scheme emphasizes play-action and timing routes — WRs who run precise routes on time.

physical_profile.height_range: 5-11 to 6-3 (broad range — Bech at 6-1, Thornton unknown)
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Not a pure speed position for this offense. Route-running and hands matter more than 40 time. However, took Thornton who led nation in yards per catch (25.4) — willing to add speed on Day 3.

playing_style.primary_style: Complete, competitive receivers who can work all levels. Route technicians who operate within timing-based offense.
least_important_traits: Pure deep threat speed (nice Day 3 add, not required for premium picks)
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite hands with competitive fire and route-running — the Tampa Bay WR profile (Chris Godwin, Mike Evans, Jalen McMillan)

college_production.production_floor: Bech had 1,034 yards and 9 TDs as breakout senior — high production preferred for Day 2 WRs
college_production.production_trajectory: Breakout senior seasons acceptable (Bech was this)

examples: 2025 2.58 — Jack Bech, WR, TCU: 6-1, 214. Physical, savvy route runner. 1,034 yards, 9 TDs. Biletnikoff semifinalist. Dropped only 1 pass all season. 'Physical and smooth, fearless over the middle, reliable hands, can block.' Senior breakout after injuries derailed earlier seasons.
2025 4.108 — Dont'e Thornton Jr., WR, Tennessee: Deep threat. Led nation in yards per catch (25.4). Career-best 661 yards. Big play potential — 6 catches of 50+ yards.
Tampa Bay precedent: Chris Godwin (3rd round — route technician), Jalen McMillan (3rd round — competitive, good hands), Bucky Irving/McMillan pairing shows Day 2-3 WR investment.

WR.confidence_note: 2 WRs drafted in first draft. Tampa Bay WR evaluation was a strength (Evans, Godwin, McMillan). Spytek's committee WR approach is a notable stated preference for 2026.

TE.archetype_summary: No TE drafted in 2025 — Brock Bowers (previous regime) is the franchise TE. Kubiak's scheme loves TEs who can block and run intermediate routes. Expect any future TE drafting to target blocking/receiving hybrids for 12-personnel packages. Tampa Bay valued TEs (traded for Gronk, drafted OJ Howard, Cade Otton). Bowers' elite talent means TE is not a 2026 draft priority.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Blocking ability and route-running for Kubiak's 12-personnel sets

playing_style.primary_style: Hybrid receiving/blocking TE for heavy personnel packages
least_important_traits: Pure move TE/slot mismatch weapon (Bowers fills this)

examples: No TE drafted in 2025. Brock Bowers is franchise TE.
Tampa Bay precedent: OJ Howard (1st round 2017), Cade Otton (4th round 2022). Licht valued blocking TEs who could catch.

TE.confidence_note: No TE draft data. Bowers' presence means TE not a priority. Any TE taken would be a Day 3 blocking complement.

RB.archetype_summary: Dynamic, do-it-all feature back with vision, elusiveness, and the ability to break tackles. The Jeanty pick at #6 is the defining data point — a generational talent who was the top RB in the class. Not afraid to invest premium capital at RB despite modern analytics suggesting otherwise. Kubiak's scheme wants a two-back system with a primary feature back and a complementary piece. Values toughness and ability to make defenders miss. Scheme fit is paramount — outside zone requires patience, vision, and decisive cutting.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Elusiveness is paramount. Jeanty forced 152 missed tackles — 50 more than any other FBS back. Burst and agility.

playing_style.primary_style: Feature back in outside-zone scheme. Must have patience, vision, and decisive cutting ability.
least_important_traits: Pure straight-line speed — vision and burst more important
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Generational elusiveness combined with power — Jeanty was 'hard to tackle' per Carroll

college_production.production_floor: Elite production — Jeanty led the nation in rushing and was a Heisman finalist

examples: 2025 1.6 — Ashton Jeanty, RB, Boise State: 5-9, 211. Led nation in rushing. 152 missed tackles forced (50 more than any other FBS back). Heisman finalist. Spytek/Carroll called him 'dynamic' with ability to 'run with power.' Pete Carroll's influence was major here.

Tampa Bay precedent: Bucky Irving (2024, 4th round — 1,122 yards as rookie). Tampa traditionally took RBs on Day 2-3, not Round 1. Jeanty pick appears influenced by Carroll more than Spytek's Tampa training.

RB.confidence_note: 1 RB drafted but at pick 6 — very high investment. Carroll's influence on the Jeanty pick was significant. Kubiak wants a two-back system, so a complementary Day 3 RB is expected in 2026.

reach_tolerance.notes: Limited data (1 draft). Jeanty at 6 was consensus value — not a reach. Jack Bech at 58 was approximately at consensus range for late 2nd. Darien Porter at 68 was projected as a 3rd-4th rounder pre-combine, but combine performance boosted him into early-3rd range — slight reach based on pre-combine boards, but post-combine boards had him in this range. Tampa Bay under Licht was comfortable reaching — Spytek explicitly said 'whether or not other people think that we drafted guys a little bit too high, I think the proof was in the pudding.' This suggests moderate reach tolerance when the character and traits are right. Small-school picks (Grant from William & Mary) also suggest comfort going off-consensus.

scheme_fit.offensive_scheme: Kubiak's Shanahan-tree West Coast offense: Wide zone run game foundation, heavy play-action, bootlegs, 12/22 personnel packages. Uses fullback. Two-back system. Motion-heavy. Timing-based passing game. Requires athletic, movement-oriented OL. TE is a key position. Run game sets up everything. Rick Dennison as OL coach reinforces zone-blocking identity.
scheme_fit.defensive_scheme: Switching to 3-4 base under new DC Rob Leonard (promoted from DL/run game coordinator). Previously ran 4-3 under Patrick Graham. This scheme change affects positional value significantly — more LB investment needed, EDGE players who can stand up and drop, versatile DL who can play multiple spots.
scheme_fit.scheme_change_notes: MAJOR scheme change on both sides. New HC (Kubiak), new OC (Janocko), new DC (Leonard), switching from 4-3 to 3-4 on defense, switching from Chip Kelly's spread offense to Kubiak's Shanahan-tree zone-run system on offense. This is a complete schematic reset. Draft tendencies from the 2025 Carroll/Kelly regime should be partially discounted. The 2026 draft will be the first reflecting Kubiak/Leonard's preferences. However, Spytek's core evaluation principles (character, competitiveness, positional value, trench play) should persist regardless of scheme.

psychological_profile.risk_description: Moderate. Willing to bet on traits over production (Porter CB pick), take small-school players (Grant from William & Mary), and draft late-bloomers. But also took consensus BPA at pick 6 (Jeanty) and a proven producer at 58 (Bech). Tampa Bay under Licht was moderate-risk: willing to bet on undersized DTs (Kancey) but always anchored by character evaluation. Spytek's Day 3 picks showed willingness to take creative risks (FCS QBs, a 300-lb wildcat QB playing DT).
psychological_profile.recency_description: High recency response. After worst rushing attack in NFL (2024), took RB at #6. After league-worst OL in 2025, multiple sources indicate OL is top 2026 priority. After QB carousel (10 different starters since 2022), taking Mendoza #1 overall. This is partly rational need-filling, but the pattern suggests the previous season's failures heavily influence the next draft.
psychological_profile.sunk_cost_behavior: Willing to move on from previous regime's investments. Traded Jakobi Meyers (signed by previous GM) mid-season for draft picks. Fired Pete Carroll after 1 season. Replaced entire coaching staff. Did not hold onto players out of loyalty to prior administration.
psychological_profile.combine_description: Moderate-to-high combine influence. The Darien Porter pick is the key evidence — Porter was projected 3rd-4th round before the combine but his historic athletic testing (9.99 RAS, 4.30 40, 6-3) clearly boosted him on Spytek's board. Spytek's Tampa tree has history of valuing athletic testing (Kancey's elite burst, Diaby's explosiveness). Spytek described himself as a 'film junkie' but the Porter pick suggests combine testing can meaningfully move players up his board when traits are elite. Also cited Hemingway and Pegues 'testing well' as positives.
psychological_profile.character_description: Very high. Character is Spytek's #1 stated and demonstrated priority. He explicitly said 'We don't miss on the player, we miss on the person' (quoting Licht's philosophy he internalized). He is 'uncompromising' on love of football, competitive spirit, humility, and willingness to play hurt. The 'I Am That Man' evaluation system from Tampa Bay was built around character-first drafting. Spytek said 'Somebody that doesn't love football... those guys just don't do it for me.' He also said a bad interview typically won't hurt a player's grade, but medical red flags will. He has stated finding 'driven, competitive, high emotionally intelligent' players is harder than finding athletes. The Brady influence reinforces this — Brady demands preparation and discipline standards for QBs especially. Cam Miller was cited as fitting the 'bring in winners' philosophy.
psychological_profile.quarterback_philosophy: Franchise QB is the ultimate prize. With the #1 pick in 2026, expected to take Fernando Mendoza. However, Spytek is not in favor of 'running a young QB out there right away' — wants to limit pressure on rookie, have a veteran mentor, build strong OL and run game first. Brady's influence means the QB evaluation bar is extraordinarily high: preparation, process, leadership matter as much as arm talent. In 2025, took two Day 3 QBs (Mellott, Miller) — development bets, not starters. Brady reportedly endorsed Miller. For Spytek, QB is an 'all-in or developmental flier' position — no mid-round starters.

college_production_preferences.production_vs_projection: Leans slightly toward projection/traits over raw production, but production is a baseline requirement at most positions. Porter (CB) is the extreme example — elite traits, minimal production, taken over more productive corners. Bech (WR) is the opposite — proven producer with 1,034 yards. Jeanty was both elite production AND elite traits. Tampa Bay's evolution under Licht/Spytek went from 'drafting athletes' early to a balance of production and traits — Spytek helped drive that shift. The key filter is character and love of football first, then traits, then production.

production_thresholds_by_position.WR: Bech had 1,034 yards — suggests preference for 1,000+ yard producers in Day 2. Thornton (Day 3) had only 661 yards — lower bar on Day 3.
production_thresholds_by_position.RB: Jeanty was the nation's leading rusher — elite production expected for premium RB investment
production_thresholds_by_position.EDGE: No clear threshold — Hemingway had 9.5 sacks over 3 years (not dominant single-season)
production_thresholds_by_position.CB: No production threshold — Porter had 3 INTs total. Traits trump stats at CB.
production_thresholds_by_position.QB: Winning matters more than raw stats — Miller won FCS championships

college_production_preferences.notes: Spytek explicitly stated older prospects 'don't deter him' — age is not a disqualifier. His analytics department pushes back on older prospects but he overrides them if the player loves football. Porter was 24, Bech was a senior breakout, Miller won championships at FCS level. Late bloomers are welcome if traits and character are right.

CB.40_yard_max: No confirmed max, but Porter at 4.30 suggests extreme speed preference. All evidence points to 4.45 or faster as strong preference
CB.min_height: 6-0 (based on Porter at 6-3 and Tampa CB history — Carlton Davis 6-1, Hargreaves 5-10 was considered small)

EDGE.notes: No confirmed thresholds from own picks. Tampa precedent: versatility > any single measurable

WR.notes: No clear athletic threshold. Bech was not a burner but had elite hands and route-running. Thornton was a deep threat. Wide spectrum acceptable.

OT.notes: Zone scheme demands movement skills over raw size

DT.notes: Sub-5.0 40 cited positively for Hemingway. Pegues valued for 'unique athletic traits' at 300+ lbs. Athleticism-for-size ratio matters.

measurable_thresholds.threshold_type: Mostly soft preferences with occasional hard cutoffs at premium positions. The Porter pick suggests Spytek has very high athletic bars at CB — he took a near-perfect athlete over more polished players. At other positions, traits are weighted but not as rigidly.

size_preferences_by_position.CB: Prefers 6-0+ with long arms (33"+) — length and speed combination is the priority
size_preferences_by_position.DT: Wide range — comfortable with 280-lb penetrators (Kancey precedent) and 340-lb run-stuffers (Vea precedent)
size_preferences_by_position.WR: No strong size preference — Bech at 6-1/214 and Thornton provide different body types

measurable_thresholds.athletic_testing_favorites: Combine testing matters most at CB (Porter's 9.99 RAS was clearly a board-mover) and DL (sub-5.0 for interior linemen cited as positive). Vertical and broad jump valued for explosiveness across positions. 3-cone and short shuttle likely weighted for EDGE and LB per Tampa Bay tendencies.
measurable_thresholds.measurables_vs_film: Film first overall — Spytek describes himself as a 'film junkie' and said 'we matched quality tape with the quality person.' However, elite combine testing CAN move players significantly up the board (Porter). When film and testing align, that's the sweet spot. When they conflict: film wins for most positions, but at CB, testing appears to carry unusually heavy weight.

round_strategy.round_1_strategy: Take the best available player who addresses a critical need AND fits character standards. Jeanty at 6 was both clear BPA at the position and biggest offensive need. In 2026, Mendoza at #1 will be QB-locked. Tampa Bay's Round 1 history: premium positions (OT, DT, LB, CB) with strong character profiles. Spytek stated he values 'understanding the value of certain positions' — expect premium position targeting in Round 1.
round_strategy.round_2_3_strategy: Active trader. Traded down twice in Round 2 to accumulate Day 2/3 picks. This is where need-filling intensifies. Comfortable taking developmental players (Porter) and scheme-specific fits (Rogers, Grant for OL). This is the sweet spot for Spytek — he believed 'the value was in Rounds 2 and 3' in 2025. Expect to see more trading and more picks in this range. Character filter still applies.
round_strategy.day_3_strategy: Developmental athletes, versatility plays, and unique skill sets. Took two FCS QBs (developmental), a 300-lb DT who also plays wildcat QB (Pegues — unique), and a Senior Bowl LB (Lindenberg — character). Day 3 is where Spytek's reputation for finding 'guys that maybe weren't on everybody's radar' should show. Matt Russell (Denver colleague) said Spytek excels at late-round gems — 'guys that might go against the grain.' Special teams contributors also targeted (Porter had 5 blocked kicks).
round_strategy.compensatory_pick_usage: Took both FCS QBs with comp picks (213, 215) — willing to use comp picks for higher-risk developmental plays. Consistent with Tampa Bay approach where comp picks were sometimes used on character/upside bets.

overall.note: Too early to evaluate hit rate — only 1 season of results. Jeanty had 975 yards/5 TDs behind NFL's worst OL. Bech contributed as a rookie.

RB.note: Jeanty produced despite poor OL — 975 yards on 266 carries. 3.7 YPC behind worst OL in NFL. Promising.

WR.note: Bech contributed as rookie starter. Thornton as deep threat. TBD.

CB.note: Porter was extremely raw with 7 college starts. Multi-year development needed.

OL.note: Rogers and Grant were development picks. Rogers expected at guard.

DT.note: Hemingway and Pegues were rotational pieces. Low draft capital spent.

blind_spots: Unknown — insufficient data with 1 draft class. Tampa Bay under Licht/Spytek had occasional misses at OLB and CB (Hargreaves bust, some late-round misses). Monitor CB evaluation given the extreme traits-over-production bet on Porter.
strengths: Tampa Bay's draft hit rate at OL (Wirfs, Barton, Mauch) and DT (Vea, Kancey) was exceptional. If Spytek's evaluation at these positions carries over, expect continued strength. WR evaluation also strong in Tampa (Evans, Godwin, McMillan). Late-round gem-finding is a reported strength per Denver and Tampa colleagues.

free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_spending_philosophy: Build through the draft as the primary philosophy, supplement through FA. Spytek explicitly said 'it takes discipline to stick to the plan and reward your own players, but I really think that's the best way to do it.' However, inherited a barren roster with $100M+ in cap space and massive needs — 2026 will require significant FA spending to fill holes the draft alone cannot address. Traded for Geno Smith (FA-adjacent move) and signed various FAs in 2025. The Tampa model was conservative in FA spending except for targeted veteran additions (Brady, Suh, Jensen, Barrett).
free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_draft_correlation: When a veteran starter is acquired at a position, Spytek still drafts developmental players there — took OL on Day 2 despite having Kolton Miller and JPJ. Doesn't completely close a position after a FA signing. Likely will address OL in both FA and draft in 2026.
free_agency_draft_interaction.roster_construction_philosophy: Draft-and-develop core with targeted FA supplementation. From Tampa: 44 of 53 players on 2024 roster were drafted or signed as UDFAs by Tampa Bay. Spytek aims to replicate this model in Las Vegas over time. In the short term, aggressive FA spending is necessary to build the foundation around Kubiak's scheme, then shift to draft-centric maintenance.

notable_patterns: Traded down twice in Round 2 to accumulate additional Day 2/3 picks — clear preference for volume
7 of 11 picks were offensive players (RB, 2 WR, 2 OL, 2 QB) — loaded up on offense in first draft
4 of last 5 picks addressed the trenches on both sides — OL/DL emphasis
Took a generational RB at #6 despite modern analytics discouraging premium RB investment — influenced by Carroll
Both QBs taken were FCS players on Day 3 — developmental approach to non-franchise QBs
Only 1 defensive player (Porter) taken before Round 4
Willingness to take small-school/FCS players (Grant from William & Mary, Mellott from Montana State, Miller from NDSU)
Athletic testing clearly influenced CB selection (Porter's 9.99 RAS)
Character and 'love of football' repeatedly cited as primary evaluation criteria

biggest_reaches.consensus_rank: 99th per Dane Brugler pre-combine, likely 70-90 post-combine
biggest_reaches.reach_amount: 0-20 picks depending on post-combine board used
biggest_reaches.context: Bypassed more polished CBs (Revel, Williams) for Porter's historic athletic profile (9.99 RAS). Only 7 career starts. Clear traits-over-production bet. Carroll's Seattle influence (long, physical corners for Cover 3) likely a factor.

biggest_steals.context: Senior Bowl standout who fell to Round 7. Good run defender with high character — exactly the profile Spytek values.

trade_history.description: Traded 2025 3rd round pick (92 overall) to Seahawks for QB Geno Smith
trade_history.analysis: Aggressive move to acquire a veteran starter. Gave up a mid-3rd rounder for a 35-year-old QB — short-term roster building.

trade_history.description: Traded 2nd round pick (37) and 5th (143) to Dolphins for 2nd (48), 3rd (98), and 4th (135)
trade_history.analysis: Classic trade-down to accumulate picks. Moved back 11 spots in Round 2 and gained a 3rd and 4th. Strong value.

trade_history.description: Traded 2nd round pick (48) to Texans for 2nd (58) and 3rd (99)
trade_history.analysis: Second consecutive trade-down. Moved back 10 spots in Round 2 and gained a 3rd. Turned 2 picks into 4 picks on Day 2.

trade_history.description: Traded Jakorian Bennett to Eagles for Thomas Booker
Traded 2026 5th round pick to Browns for QB Kenny Pickett
trade_history.analysis: Acquired veteran QB depth for a late-round pick. Low-cost insurance.

trade_history.description: Traded Jakobi Meyers to Jaguars for 2026 4th and 6th round picks
trade_history.analysis: Sold veteran WR (previous regime signing) for future draft capital during lost season. Smart asset management.

confidence_notes: Only 1 draft as Raiders GM, and that draft was conducted with a different HC (Pete Carroll, now fired) and different OC (Chip Kelly, now gone). The 2026 draft under Kubiak's scheme will be far more indicative of Spytek's long-term tendencies. Tampa Bay scouting tree (Licht) provides the strongest behavioral baseline. Character-first evaluation and draft-and-develop philosophy are high-confidence predictions. Specific positional archetypes and trade behavior will become clearer after the 2026 draft. The scheme change from 4-3 to 3-4 on defense and from spread to Shanahan zone-run on offense complicates archetype predictions. Profile should be heavily refreshed after the 2026 draft.
